questions: Identify the specific tier of the U.S. federal court system comprising 13 courts that sit immediately below the Supreme Court. Detail the specific prohibitions these courts have regarding witness testimony and the hearing of new evidence. Additionally, for the supervision officers working at the district level, detail the specific 'Risk-Need-Responsivity' model practices: list the acronyms for the risk assessment tools used for pretrial versus post-conviction individuals, and define the specific focus of the 'Responsivity' principle.

rounds.question: Identify the specific tier of the U.S. federal court system comprising 13 courts that sit immediately below the Supreme Court. Detail the specific prohibitions these courts have regarding witness testimony and the hearing of new evidence. Additionally, for the supervision officers working at the district level, detail the specific 'Risk-Need-Responsivity' model practices: list the acronyms for the risk assessment tools used for pretrial versus post-conviction individuals, and define the specific focus of the 'Responsivity' principle.
checklist_width: Appellate Court Prohibition 1: They do not hear witnesses testify.
Appellate Court Prohibition 2: They do not hear new evidence (or retry cases).
Pretrial Tool Acronym: PTRA (Pretrial Risk Assessment).
Post-Conviction Tool Acronym: PCRA (Post Conviction Risk Assessment).
Responsivity Principle Definition: Focuses on reducing barriers specific to an individual’s unique circumstances (e.g., transportation, childcare, lack of motivation).
checklist_depth: Target Entity 1: U.S. Courts of Appeals (Identified via logic '13 courts below Supreme Court').
Target Entity 2: Federal Probation and Pretrial Services / Evidence-Based Practices (Identified via logic 'supervision officers... district level').
rounds.rationale: The question requires Deep reasoning to identify the 'U.S. Courts of Appeals' based solely on their hierarchical position (13 courts below the Supreme Court) without naming them. It then requires Wide aggregation by forcing the user to retrieve procedural details about those courts (Target 1) and combine them with specific acronyms and definitions from the separate 'Evidence-Based Practices' text (Target 0) regarding probation supervision.
source_nodes: About the U.S. Courts of Appeals
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Evidence-Based Practices Probation and pretrial services officers are committed to using evidence-based practices (EBP) to promote public safety and positively impact the live
rounds.answer_a:
The U.S. federal court system includes the United States Courts of Appeals as the intermediate appellate tier, comprising 13 courts (12 regional circuits and one Federal Circuit) that sit immediately below the Supreme Court [1]. These courts primarily review decisions from the district courts, the U.S. Tax Court, and certain federal agencies, focusing on legal errors rather than retrying cases.

Regarding prohibitions, the Courts of Appeals are strictly limited in handling new evidence and witness testimony. They do not permit the introduction of new evidence that was not presented at the trial level, as their role is to evaluate the existing record for errors of law, fact, or procedure [2]. Similarly, these courts prohibit live witness testimony; they rely solely on transcripts, briefs, and oral arguments from attorneys, without conducting trials or hearing direct witness accounts [3]. This ensures appellate review remains focused on the lower court's proceedings, promoting judicial efficiency and finality.

For supervision officers, such as probation and pretrial services officers working at the district court level, the Risk-Need-Responsivity (RNR) model guides offender supervision practices [4]. This evidence-based approach assesses and addresses offender risks to reduce recidivism. The risk assessment tools differ by stage: for pretrial individuals, the acronym is PTRA (Pretrial Risk Assessment), which evaluates flight risk and danger to the community [5]. For post-conviction individuals, it is PCRA (Post-Conviction Risk Assessment), focusing on recidivism likelihood based on criminal history and other factors [6].

The Responsivity principle specifically emphasizes tailoring interventions to the individual's unique characteristics, such as learning style, motivation, cognitive abilities, cultural background, and barriers like mental health or language issues, to maximize treatment effectiveness [7]. This principle ensures programs are responsive and engaging, rather than applying a one-size-fits-all approach.

(Word count: 278)

## References
[1] United States Courts. "Court Role and Structure." https://www.uscourts.gov/about-federal-courts/court-role-and-structure  
[2] Legal Information Institute. "Appellate Procedure." https://www.law.cornell.edu/wex/appellate_procedure  
[3] Federal Judicial Center. "Appellate Courts Overview." https://www.fjc.gov/subject/appellate-courts  
[4] Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. "Probation and Pretrial Services." https://www.uscourts.gov/services-forms/probation-and-pretrial-services  
[5] Pretrial Justice Institute. "Pretrial Risk Assessment Tools." https://www.pretrial.org/  
[6] National Institute of Corrections. "Post-Conviction Risk Assessment." https://nicic.gov/post-conviction-risk-assessment-pcra  
[7] Bonta, J., & Andrews, D. A. (2017). "The Psychology of Criminal Conduct." Routledge. (Summary via https://www.publicsafety.gc.ca/cnt/rsrcs/pblctns/rsk-nd-rspnsvty/index-en.aspx)
rounds.answer_b:
The tier of the U.S. federal court system comprising 13 courts that sit immediately below the Supreme Court is the U.S. Courts of Appeals, also known as circuit courts [1]. These 13 appellate courts do not conduct trials and are prohibited from hearing new evidence or receiving witness testimony; their role is limited to reviewing decisions made by lower courts for legal errors [2]. Appellate courts rely on the trial court record, written briefs, and oral arguments to assess whether the law was correctly applied [3].

At the district level, supervision officers employ the Risk-Need-Responsivity (RNR) model to guide supervision and rehabilitation practices [4]. For risk assessment, different tools are used based on the stage of the legal process. Pretrial individuals are assessed using the Public Safety Assessment (PSA), which evaluates flight risk and danger to the community [5]. Post-conviction individuals are typically evaluated with the Level of Service Inventory-Revised (LSI-R) or its updated version, the Level of Service/Case Management Inventory (LS/CMI), which assess criminogenic needs and recidivism risk [6].

A key component of the RNR model is the "Responsivity" principle, which mandates that interventions be delivered in a style and mode that match the individual’s learning abilities, motivation, culture, and personal characteristics [7]. The principle emphasizes cognitive-behavioral treatment methods and tailoring interventions to maximize effectiveness, ensuring that the delivery of services enhances an individual’s receptiveness to rehabilitation [8].

## References  
[1] United States Courts. "About the Federal Courts." https://www.uscourts.gov  
[2] Federal Judicial Center. "Appellate Courts." https://www.fjc.gov  
[3] Cornell Law School Legal Information Institute. "Appellate Court." https://www.law.cornell.edu  
[4] Bonta, J., & Andrews, D. A. (2017). "The Psychology of Criminal Conduct."  
[5] Laura and John Arnold Foundation. "Public Safety Assessment." https://www.arnoldfoundation.org  
[6] Andrews, D. A., et al. (1990). "The Level of Service Inventory-Revised."  
[7] Andrews, D. A., & Bonta, J. (2003). "The Psychology of Criminal Conduct."  
[8] Taxman, F. S. (2008). "Advancing Supervision through Risk-Need-Responsivity." DOJ.
rounds.judge_reasoning: The prompt specifically asks about the U.S. federal court system. While both agents correctly identified the Courts of Appeals and their prohibitions, Agent A correctly identified the specific risk assessment tools used by federal probation and pretrial services officers (PTRA and PCRA). Agent B provided tools commonly used in state or local jurisdictions (PSA, LSI-R), which is factually incorrect for the specific federal context requested.
